title: On some systems, running `sleep 1` can take up to 8s when running several processes in parallel
body:
### Issue Description

Hi, we are seeing an issue in podman that running several concurrent instances of `podman run busybox sleep 1` exhibits the following behavior:

- At first, each sleep command takes ~1.2-1.3s, which is expected (since podman has some small overhead).
- Over time though, the performance degrades. The average slowly goes up to about 2s per sleep command, with many commands taking 3-4s and a few commands taking as much as 8s.

(Our real problem is that we have a service that runs podman containers on-the-fly, and occasionally we see the tail latency spike up very high when running several containers concurrently. But this `sleep` example is probably easier to look at since it mostly takes the container workload type out of the equation.)

I am running Ubuntu 22.04 with podman 4.6.2 (from the [alvistack](https://software.opensuse.org/download/package?package=podman&project=home%3Aalvistack) repo) but am also able to reproduce this with earlier versions of podman, including 4.5. The number of concurrent instances is limited to the number of available threads on my machine (32) by using `make -j32`.

Oddly, I *cannot* reproduce this behavior when running podman 4.6.2 nested inside an alpine linux container. All of the runs seem to be consistently taking ~1.2-1.3s without getting sluggish. I'm running the alpine container on my ubuntu machine like `podman run --privileged mgoltzsche/podman:4.6.2`.

I was also able to reproduce this on a Debian 11 VM with `gcloud compute instances create` with settings `--machine-type=c2-standard-30 -boot-disk-size=100GB --boot-disk-type=pd-ssd`

I am happy to help gather any profiling data needed to find the bottleneck here, I just don't have a good sense for what to look for yet. I don't see any obvious differences in `top` or `iotop` when running the repro script in both setups. And it is hard to make sense of the `strace` output because I am running so many tasks in parallel; it outputs data too quickly.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Install `make` and `bc` if not installed (`sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install make bc`), then copy and paste the following into a terminal (if needed, replace `32` in `make -j32` with the number of CPU cores on your system)

```shell
# Write a script "sleep1.sh" that runs `sleep 1` in a busybox container with podman
echo > ./sleep1.sh '#!/bin/sh
set -eu
start=$(cat /proc/uptime | awk "{print \$1}")
podman run --net=none --rm mirror.gcr.io/library/busybox sleep 1
end=$(cat /proc/uptime | awk "{print \$1}")
echo $(echo $end - $start | bc) seconds
'
chmod +x ./sleep1.sh

# Write a Makefile that when running make <anything> it just runs sleep1.sh
# e.g. "make 1 2 3" will just run sleep1.sh 3 times
printf '%%:\n\t./sleep1.sh\n' > Makefile

# Pull busybox
podman pull mirror.gcr.io/library/busybox

# Run `sleep1.sh` 1000 times, with 32 concurrent jobs
# Replace 32 with the number of cores on your system
seq 1 1000 | xargs sudo make -j32
```

### Describe the results you received

- At first, each sleep command takes 1.3s, which is fine - I expect around 300ms of podman overhead.
- Over time though, the performance degrades. It looks to be about 2s on average, with frequent spikes as high as 8s.


### Describe the results you expected

Each command should take 1.3-1.4s consistently, like it does in Alpine.

### podman info output

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out and providing the nice reproducer, @bduffany.

I think you are reaching the architectural limitations of Podman.  Podman does not run as a daemon but employs a traditional fork-exec model for running containers.  That implies that state must be written to and synchronized on disk.  If we have 32 processes running concurrently and all wanting to remove the container, they will queue in on the lock for the central container storage.  This storage is on disk, and for each write/removal Podman needs to perform a fairly expensive fsync call.

That means that the `podman run --rm` processes queue at the file locks, waiting for the previous ones to finish certain operations.  Podman is not designed for such highly concurrent use cases.  _BUT_ there a tricks :-)  Can you try running `podman --transient-store` (see "Transient Store" in https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/speed-containers-podman-raspberry-pi) and observe the behavior?  That should _greatly_ improve the situation as many operations are significantly faster; especially the fsyncs have near 0 cost.

@vrothberg thanks for replying so quickly! Seems `--transient-store` fixes the issue. For the repro I was using, it turns out that setting both `--transient-store` and `--net=none` is needed to get good performance (as they mention in that article). If I remove either of those, I see the throughput issues.
@bduffany, thanks for reporting back.  I am glad it's performing in your use case.  Please reach out again if you encounter any other issue.
